skill.name: api-design-principles
skill.description: Master REST and GraphQL API design principles to build intuitive, scalable, and maintainable APIs that delight developers.
skill.summary: Educational skill for API design best practices covering REST architecture, GraphQL schema design, versioning strategies, error handling, and security considerations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 201 patterns that are all false positives. The skill contains only documentation and educational content about API design. Markdown code blocks (backticks) were incorrectly identified as shell execution. Cryptographic references appear in security checklists, not actual implementations. URL and IP references are documentation examples for API endpoints. No executable code or security risks present.

content.value_statement: Building APIs without consistent design principles leads to confusing interfaces and poor developer experience. This skill provides proven REST and GraphQL patterns to create maintainable, well-documented APIs that scale with your needs.
actual_capabilities: Guide REST API resource naming and URL structure decisions
Design GraphQL schemas with proper type relationships and interfaces
Recommend pagination, filtering, and sorting strategies
Define error response formats and HTTP status code usage
Establish API versioning and deprecation policies
Review API specifications for consistency and best practices
limitations: Does not generate executable API implementation code
Does not replace API testing or security auditing tools
Requires user to adapt patterns to specific framework constraints
Focuses on design principles rather than infrastructure setup

use_cases.title: Greenfield API Design
use_cases.description: Design a new REST or GraphQL API from scratch with proper resource modeling, endpoint structure, and documentation standards before implementation begins.
use_cases.target_user: Backend developers and architects starting new projects

use_cases.title: API Review and Refactoring
use_cases.description: Evaluate existing API endpoints against design principles to identify inconsistencies, improve naming conventions, and plan migration strategies.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering leads managing legacy API modernization

use_cases.title: Team Standards Documentation
use_cases.description: Establish organization-wide API design guidelines covering versioning, error handling, authentication patterns, and response formats for team alignment.
use_cases.target_user: Technical writers and platform teams creating internal standards

prompt_templates.title: Basic REST Endpoint Design
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to design REST endpoints for a blog system with authors, posts, and comments. Help me structure the URL hierarchy, HTTP methods, and response formats following REST best practices.

prompt_templates.title: GraphQL Schema Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my GraphQL schema for a user management system. Check for proper use of non-null types, interface implementations, and suggest improvements for query efficiency and avoid N+1 problems.

prompt_templates.title: API Versioning Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: My API has breaking changes coming. Compare URL path versioning, header versioning, and query parameter approaches for my use case. Include deprecation timeline recommendations.

prompt_templates.title: Error Handling Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a consistent error response format for my REST API. Include field-level validation errors, error codes for programmatic handling, and appropriate HTTP status codes for common scenarios.

output_examples.input: Design endpoints for an e-commerce product catalog
What HTTP status codes for common scenarios?
output: 200 OK - Successful GET, PUT, PATCH requests
201 Created - Successful POST with resource creation
204 No Content - Successful DELETE or empty response
400 Bad Request - Malformed JSON or invalid syntax
403 Forbidden - Valid auth but insufficient permissions
404 Not Found - Resource does not exist
429 Too Many Requests - Rate limit exceeded
500 Internal Server Error - Unexpected server failure

best_practices: Use plural nouns for resource collections and keep URL hierarchies shallow (maximum 2-3 levels of nesting)
Implement consistent pagination with documented page size limits and include total count metadata in responses
Version APIs from the start using URL path or header versioning, and maintain a clear deprecation policy for older versions
anti_patterns: Using verbs in endpoints like /createUser or /getUserById instead of proper HTTP methods on resource nouns
Returning different response formats or field names across similar endpoints, breaking client expectations
Deeply nested URLs like /users/{id}/orders/{orderId}/items/{itemId}/reviews that couple resources too tightly

faq.question: Should I use REST or GraphQL for my new API?
faq.answer: Choose REST when you have clear resource boundaries, need strong caching, or serve simple CRUD operations. Choose GraphQL when clients need flexible data fetching, you have complex relationships, or mobile clients need to minimize requests.

faq.question: How do I handle API versioning without breaking existing clients?
faq.answer: Start versioning from v1 at launch. For breaking changes, create v2 alongside v1. Document deprecation timelines (typically 6-12 months). Use sunset headers to notify clients of upcoming removals. Never remove versions without sufficient notice.

faq.question: What pagination approach should I use for large datasets?
faq.answer: Use offset-based pagination (page/page_size) for simple use cases with datasets under 100k records. Use cursor-based pagination for large datasets or real-time data where consistency matters. Always enforce maximum page sizes to prevent abuse.

faq.question: How do I design error responses that developers can actually use?
faq.answer: Include a machine-readable error code, human-readable message, and optional field-level details. Use consistent structure across all endpoints. Document all error codes in your API reference. Return validation errors as 422 with field-specific messages.

faq.question: What rate limiting strategy should I implement?
faq.answer: Start with requests per minute per API key or IP. Include rate limit headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset). Return 429 status with Retry-After header when exceeded. Consider tiered limits based on authentication level.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication in API design?
faq.answer: Use Bearer tokens (JWT or opaque) in Authorization headers for most APIs. Consider API keys for server-to-server communication. Never accept credentials in URL parameters. Document token refresh flows clearly. Return 401 for authentication failures, 403 for authorization failures.
